So, if you draw those two trend lines on a chart, you eventually, and it, many businesses doesn't take long, before the cost of getting people through the doors is so great in proportion to the sale that is being made that they cannot stay in business. And mid-range restaurants right now, perfect example, right? So Friday, Friday's announced last week they're selling all 276 of their restaurants to independent franchise owners and they're not going to be in the restaurant business. It's because of this chart. It's because of this chart. If you do business online, same thing, the cost of getting qualified eyeballs to a site is going up by a greater ratio faster than is the inflation embedded in what it is that you sell. So, in any business, the chart exists. We draw it a little different industry by industry, but it exists. And so, the inevitable result of that chart, regardless of what business you're in, is depth unless you fix it. So you can fix it by somehow bringing those costs back down, which everybody works on a lot, right? Everybody works on that a lot. Log mailers are trying to figure out how to trim the page size to six by nine instead of eight by ten. And we can say, okay, so everybody works on this a lot, right? Or you can try and fix it by better monetizing the people you do get to walk through the doors. And that's not entirely sales-based in retail. It's signage, traffic pattern, merchandising, et cetera. It is largely sales-based. And when you are creating leads and handing them to salespeople, this chart prohibits handing them to poor salespeople. Now it even prohibits handing them to mediocre salespeople. You can only afford to hand a good lead to a really good salesperson or a really good sales manager who then makes sure he's managing really good salespeople. And if you aren't working on that, all the work you do over here is band-aids over cancer. The chart's the same. It just takes longer to get forward to put you on the steps of bankruptcy court. Got you. Got you. And I remind everybody that we are going to be getting into step-by-step on how to solve all of these problems. But we have to lay the foundations first before we get into the actual techniques. So Nick, one of the things that you teach your guys and what you teach other salespeople is that there's a big difference between the consultative type of sale and the transactional sale. And by the way, thank you for the pen. And did you notice that, Dan? He took my pen along the way. Yeah, well, look at it. I mean, you know. It's a price elasticity. What's the difference between the two pens? One sold for 500. The other one sold for, you know, 50 cents. And they both right. And they both right. And they both right. Frankly. You're like, yours better? Mine better. There you go. There you go. So what is the difference between that, the transactional sale and the consultative sale? Sure. The consultative sale was something that was coined in the 70s by a gentleman by the name of Mar canna. And really it is a diagnostic interview. It's a step-by-step process. It's a series of questions that's meant to get to a benefit or an outcome. And it's a process that is put in place. And you really want to script it. You really want to have it move along the way. Because it allows the salesperson to be ever present in the conversation with the customer or the prospect. A transactional sale is really something that is like what we talked about the Panera. It's quick. It's easy. Usually the person is coming into the store. It may be price driven. But by having a system and a process, you could raise the average price. You could raise the average transaction level on both. And I'll use one example that could go both ways. So when I get back from this taping, I got to go get my son a suit for his homecoming dance. Because you are the fashion play. So you want him to be the fashion play to GKIC. So you want him to make sure he has a pocket square like Dan pointed out. But when you go there, these... This is for his prom. Homecoming. Homecoming. Pocket square up here. Condom down here. Absolutely. Absolutely. Just make sure you take care of that. Both ways. I was wondering what something was going to happen. So but they'll have a process, right? They'll have a process for the sale. They'll have the suit, the tie, two ties, socks, underwear. There's a process. When I was wearing suits more on the day to day, I got my suits made. He had a process also. Well, there's two different sales. But one is more consultive. You know, what do you do? What are you doing? Are you sitting a lot? You know, are you in and out of cars? Are you in front of clients? What type of clients are you in front of?
